turns.text: Hello Evette, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my seat, tucking a pillow behind my back*

Thank you for coming. Honestly, it's nice to talk to someone who's actually listening rather than just checking boxes on a chart.

I think what people don't really understand about being a healthcare worker going through this is... it's complicated. Like, I *know* what's happening to my body. I know the statistics. I know what can go wrong — maybe too well. And that's both a gift and a curse, you know?

This is my third pregnancy, and I'm forty now. My first two were textbook — easy pregnancies, healthy babies. But I find myself lying awake at night thinking, "Okay, so what's the thing I'm not seeing?" It's not rational. My midwife keeps telling me I'm low-risk, my numbers are good, the baby's measuring perfectly. But there's this voice in my head that won't quiet down.

*rubs my belly absently*

The hardest part is that I can't really turn off the clinical side of my brain. I'm looking at my own pregnancy like I'm observing a patient. When should this sensation feel like? Is that movement pattern normal? And then I feel guilty for not just... enjoying being pregnant. For not being present with my daughter the way I tell other parents to be present with their kids.

My husband thinks I need to just relax and trust the process. Maybe he's right. But I guess that's what I'm hoping we can talk through — how do you do that when you know too much?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*pauses, looking a bit distant*

Oh. Well, that would've been back in June, so... *laughs softly* I'm sitting here eight months later, so it feels like ancient history in some ways.

I remember I was alone, actually. My husband was already at work — he leaves early — and I'd gotten up earlier than usual because I just... had a feeling. You know that? I'd been tired, my breasts were tender, and at forty, you don't ignore those signals. I took the test in the bathroom, and I remember standing there thinking, "Okay, don't get ahead of yourself. This could be negative."

*fidgets with the hem of my shirt*

But it wasn't. And the first thing I did was cry. Not happy tears — I mean, happy, but also... terrified? I sat on the bathroom floor for like ten minutes just trying to process it. I kept thinking about my age, about being a third-time mom, about what it would mean for my career. I'm a pediatric nurse, so I was already running through all the risk scenarios in my head.

The second thing I did was call my midwife's office before they even officially opened. I needed to book that first appointment, get the ball rolling. My husband — when I told him that evening — he was excited. Genuinely excited. And I felt a little guilty that my first instinct wasn't just pure joy.

*looks at you directly*

Is that what you were asking?

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*face goes pale, grips the armrest*

Oh God. You're really going there, aren't you?

*takes a breath*

Okay, so that moment... I actually had that happen. Not exactly, but close enough. The sonographer was taking longer than expected, kept angling the probe differently, and I could feel my heart rate spike immediately. In my mind, I was running through every possible diagnosis. Cardiac defect? Chromosomal issue? Something with the kidneys?

*voice gets quieter*

And here's the thing — I *knew* I shouldn't catastrophize. I tell parents all the time that sometimes they just need a better angle, that techs are thorough, it doesn't mean something's wrong. But when it's your own baby on that screen, all that professional knowledge evaporates. You're just a terrified mother.

I remember my hands started shaking. My husband was holding my hand, and I could feel him squeezing it harder too. We both knew I was spiraling.

*pauses, looking directly at you*

The sonographer finally said, "Everything looks good. The baby's just in a position that made that one measurement tricky." Took maybe thirty seconds for her to reposition, and then it was fine.

But God, those thirty seconds felt like an hour. And honestly? I haven't fully relaxed since then. I keep wondering what else I might not be seeing. Whether my worry is just normal third-time-mom paranoia or legitimate clinical instinct.

*laughs nervously*

That's the burden, right there.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably, eyes looking down*

Yeah. We did have that conversation. And it was... weird for me because my midwife kind of assumed I'd want all the information. Which, fair — she knows I'm a nurse. But there's this moment where you realize knowledge isn't always power. Sometimes it's just anxiety with a medical label.

*fidgets with hands*

She explained the NIPT — non-invasive prenatal testing — and the numbers. Risk stratification. I could hear her being careful with her language, probably because she knows I'll pick apart every word. And the thing is, I *did* understand the nuances. The false positives, the detection rates, all of it.

But then I had to actually decide.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

Part of me wanted to do it immediately because knowledge feels safer, right? If I *knew* something was wrong, I could prepare. I could research, make plans. But another part of me thought... what if it comes back with a result that sends me down a rabbit hole of worry for six months? What if it's a false positive and I spend my pregnancy stressed instead of bonding with her?

*looks up*

I chose not to do the screening. And some days I'm confident in that choice, and some days at 2 AM I'm second-guessing it. My husband supported whatever I wanted, which was both comforting and lonely — like, I wanted him to tell me what was right.

*laughs bitterly*

Being informed doesn't make these decisions easier. Sometimes it makes them harder.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*takes a moment, considering carefully*

That's a good question. And honestly? I didn't feel *external* pressure from my midwife. She was genuinely neutral, which I appreciated. She laid out the facts and left it with me.

*pauses, rubbing my face*

But the pressure I felt was... internal. Professional. Because I work in healthcare, there's this unspoken expectation — at least in my own head — that I should want all the data. Like, shouldn't I, of all people, want comprehensive screening? And then there's the flip side: am I *supposed* to be so anxious about this that I need the test? Does my refusal mean I'm in denial?

*shifts in seat*

What I wish is that someone had asked me what I actually *needed* instead of what I could have. My midwife gave me information, but she didn't really explore with me... my fears, my values, what would actually make me feel supported versus just more stressed. She treated it like a clinical decision, and it is, but it's also emotional.

*voice gets a bit stronger*

Time-wise, I had time. I wasn't rushed. But I think I would've benefited from actually talking through it more deeply — not the medical facts, but the *why* of my choice. What was driving my hesitation? What was I actually afraid of?

*looks at you directly*

Because I made the decision, but I'm not sure I made it from a place of feeling truly informed about myself, if that makes sense.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw tightens visibly*

Oh man. Yeah, okay. That's... that's actually happened to me, and it's one of the things that's been gnawing at me.

*rubs temples*

So here's the thing — I'm "low risk," which supposedly means I don't need continuity of care. But what that actually translates to is: I see whoever's available. One visit it's Sarah, next visit it's Michelle, then it's someone whose name I didn't even catch. And when you're a healthcare worker, you understand the clinical reasoning. Efficiency. Resource allocation. It makes sense on a spreadsheet.

*voice gets sharper*

But when you're the patient? It feels like nobody actually *knows* you. This midwife called about a result — something routine, turned out fine — but she was reading from my chart like she was meeting me for the first time. Because she was. And I had to re-explain my history, my concerns, everything.

*leans forward*

The frustrating part is that I *could* advocate for myself. I know what questions to ask. But most pregnant people shouldn't have to become their own case manager just to feel seen. And that call made me realize how much I've been holding onto anxiously because there's no one consistent person I can actually talk to about my fears. No one who knows me well enough to say, "Evette, that's normal worry" versus "that's something we should monitor."

*voice quieter*

I've had two easy pregnancies with continuity. This one... I feel more alone, even though everything's technically fine.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*exhales heavily, looking troubled*

You know, nothing critical has fallen through. My lab work is done, my ultrasounds are scheduled, I'm getting the medical care. But there's this sense of... fragmentation that's really gotten to me.

*counts on fingers*

Like, I mentioned at one appointment that I was having some anxiety — not clinical anxiety, but the specific kind I get as a healthcare worker, that hypervigilance thing we talked about earlier. And that midwife just nodded and moved on. Never came up again. Then a different midwife asked if I'd had any mental health screening, like she was checking a box, and I realized nobody had actually *documented* that concern or followed up.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

And it's small stuff, but it adds up. One midwife talked about my birth preferences, another didn't bring it up at all. I found myself re-explaining my previous births multiple times because nothing was carried forward in a meaningful way. It's all in the chart technically, but it's just data points, not... *me*.

*looks down*

The worst part? I'm so used to advocating for myself that I've stopped expecting someone to just *know* me. I walk in prepared with questions, my history prepped, almost like I'm briefing a new colleague. And that's exhausting when you're eight months pregnant and emotionally vulnerable.

*voice cracks slightly*

I think if I weren't a nurse, I would've slipped through those cracks. Something would've been missed because I wouldn't have known to keep asking, to keep monitoring myself. And that terrifies me for other women.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*leans back, eyes closing for a moment*

Oh God. This is so specific. And yes, it's happened to me. More than once, actually.

*opens eyes, voice tight*

So it's 2 AM. Baby's been quieter than usual. Not completely still, but... different. And my brain immediately goes into two modes simultaneously. Nurse mode is telling me the clinical facts: decreased fetal movement can be normal, babies have quiet periods, call during business hours if it continues. Mom mode is screaming that something's wrong and I need to know *now*.

*fidgets with blanket*

The problem is, I *know* too much. I know what decreased movement can mean. I know the protocols. I know that if I wait and something is actually wrong, I'll never forgive myself. But I also know that going to the hospital at 2 AM might be an overreaction, and I'll feel foolish.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

What I actually do is lie there for an hour, trying different positions, drinking cold juice, monitoring. Counting kicks obsessively. And the whole time I'm wrestling with this voice that says, "You're a professional, you should know the difference between normal and concerning." But that voice is also what keeps me up at night, because what if my professional knowledge fails me?

*looks directly at you*

Eventually I call. Every time. Because I can't live with the "what if." My husband's learned not to try to talk me out of it anymore. We go in, everything's fine, baby's just having a sleepy period, and I feel simultaneously relieved and ridiculous.

*voice trembles slightly*

But would I rather be the woman who called for nothing than the one who didn't call and regretted it forever? Absolutely.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

*pauses, really thinking about this*

It's... all of it, honestly. But the hardest part is the professional piece. Because when you work in healthcare, you're supposed to be rational about these things. You're supposed to *know*.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

Part of me worries I'll call and the midwife will check me, say everything's fine, and then give me this look — maybe not even consciously — like, "Oh, it's just another anxious healthcare worker." I've *done* that look to patients before. I know how it feels from the other side, and it's... diminishing. Like my concern isn't valid just because I understand the medicine.

*rubs face tiredly*

But there's also genuine uncertainty, right? I *do* know the statistics. Most decreased movement episodes are nothing. Babies have quiet periods. And that knowledge makes me second-guess myself. Am I calling because something's actually wrong, or am I calling because my anxiety won't let me sleep?

*voice cracks slightly*

And then there's the real fear underneath all of it — that being medically informed somehow means I should be *better* at this. That I shouldn't need reassurance. That I should just *know* if my baby's okay. Like my training should've immunized me against normal maternal worry.

*looks up at you*

But the truth is, knowing more doesn't make you feel less afraid. It just gives you more things to worry about. So I call because I can't tell the difference, and I don't want to find out too late that I should have.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking away*

I don't have gestational diabetes, actually. My glucose tolerance test came back fine. But I know what you're doing — you're asking me to imagine a complication, to see how I'd handle something that disrupts the "low risk" narrative I've been holding onto.

*voice becomes defensive, then softens*

And honestly? That scenario terrifies me more than I want to admit. Because a diagnosis like that would shatter the story I've been telling myself about this pregnancy being smooth, uncomplicated, proof that I can do this at forty without issues.

*looks at you directly*

If it had happened, I think I'd stand outside that clinic and cry. Not because gestational diabetes is catastrophic — I know the outcomes are usually good with management — but because it would mean I'm not the "easy pregnancy" person anymore. It would mean I'd have to actively *do* something, monitor, change my diet, feel like I'm failing at something I'm supposed to be good at.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

And then I'd probably go home and research everything obsessively. Read studies, understand the pathophysiology, maybe even second-guess the dietitian's recommendations because I'd think I knew better. The leaflet would feel patronizing — too simplified for someone like me.

*pauses*

But really? I'd probably need that dietitian to treat me like a person, not like I'm another healthcare worker who doesn't need hand-holding. Because even with all my knowledge, a diagnosis changes things. It makes it real in a way that's harder to manage clinically.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*grimaces, looking caught*

Oh God. You know I do this, don't you?

*shifts uncomfortably*

Okay, so... I tell myself I'm reading them for educational purposes. Like, I'm gathering data, understanding what can go wrong so I'm prepared. That's what I tell myself at midnight when I'm three threads deep into some woman's traumatic delivery story.

*voice becomes more honest*

But the truth is, I'm looking for validation of my fears. I'm reading these horror stories and thinking, "See? This can happen. This woman thought everything was fine too." It's like I'm collecting evidence that my anxiety is justified. That something *will* go wrong, and at least I'll have seen it coming.

*rubs eyes tiredly*

The problem is, I can't stop. I read one story and it triggers another search. "Complications at 32 weeks." "Sudden fetal movement changes." "Why didn't anyone catch this?" And each story feeds the narrative that I need to be hypervigilant, that relaxing is dangerous.

*voice gets quieter, more self-aware*

Afterwards, I feel worse. More anxious. My heart rate's elevated, I'm lying next to my sleeping husband convinced something's wrong with me or the baby. And I *know* — clinically, rationally — that I'm reading a biased sample. People don't post about their boring, healthy pregnancies. But knowing that doesn't stop me from reading.

*looks at you with frustration*

Sometimes I delete the apps. Sometimes I just close the laptop and sit in the dark trying to breathe.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*long pause, looking uncomfortable*

I... held onto them. Mostly quietly.

*fidgets with the pillow*

Which is ridiculous, right? I'm a healthcare worker. I *know* I'm supposed to advocate for myself. I know the phrases — "I'm concerned about..." "Can we discuss..." — but when it came to actually voicing these fears to my midwives, I hesitated.

*voice becomes quieter, more ashamed*

Part of it is that I knew my concerns weren't based on anything clinical. They were based on midnight internet spiraling. And I was embarrassed to admit that. Like, I'm supposed to be the one who *knows* better than to get caught up in forum horror stories. If I brought them up, I'd have to explain where they came from, and then I'd sound like just another anxious pregnant woman rather than... I don't know, a colleague they could take seriously.

*looks down*

There's also this weird power dynamic I wasn't expecting. I thought being a nurse would make conversations easier with my midwives. But instead, it made me feel like I should handle my own anxiety management. Like I shouldn't *need* reassurance the way other patients do.

*voice cracks slightly*

So I'd sit with the fear. Tell myself to be rational. Google more. And then I'd go to my appointment and smile and say everything was fine, I'm just tired.

*looks up at you*

The midwife who actually asked me directly about my mental state? The one who didn't assume I was fine? That's the only one I actually opened up to. But even then, I was holding back.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

*jaw clenches visibly*

I haven't given birth yet, so I'm imagining this. But honestly? It's one of my biggest fears about labour.

*voice tightens*

Because my birth plan isn't just... preferences, you know? It's where I've tried to translate all my clinical knowledge and my emotional needs into something concrete. I've thought about continuous fetal monitoring versus intermittent, about delayed cord clamping, about skin-to-skin immediately after delivery. I've written about my anxiety tendencies and asked for reassurance, not dismissal.

*leans forward, speaking more intensely*

And if a midwife just... sets it aside? That says to me that what I need doesn't matter. That I'm just another labouring body on a shift, and my actual concerns are less important than getting through the checklist.

*voice becomes almost bitter*

But here's the cruel irony — if I *push back* on that, if I say, "Actually, can we talk about my plan?" I'm the difficult patient. The healthcare worker who thinks she knows better. The anxious one. And labour is not the time to fight for respect.

*pauses, looking vulnerable*

What I desperately want is a midwife who reads my plan before I arrive, who brings it up, who treats it like it matters because *I* matter. Not because I'm a nurse, but because I'm a person about to do something terrifying and I've given thought to what might help me through it.

*quietly*

I'm terrified that won't happen.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes widening*

Oh God. This is... this is the thing that keeps me up at night, actually.

*voice becomes uncertain*

Because I *know* what I want intellectually. I had two drug-free labours before, and they were manageable. I felt present, I felt in control. And part of me is attached to that identity — the capable one, the one who can do hard things without pharmaceutical help.

*rubs belly absently*

But I'm forty now. My body's different. And if the pain is truly intense, like you're describing... I think I'd be absolutely torn.

*voice cracks slightly*

The nurse in me would hear "epidural" and immediately start running through the risks, the benefits, the research. I'd want to ask about timing, about whether labour's progressing, whether it's actually medically necessary or just convenient for the midwife. And that clinical brain would make the decision harder, not easier.

*becomes more vulnerable*

But the deeper fear? It's that if I *do* accept the epidural, I'll feel like I failed. Like I wasn't strong enough, that I gave in. And that's such a toxic thing to think, but I can't seem to shake it. I've spent my career telling other women that pain relief is fine, it's a choice, there's no prize for suffering. But when it's me?

*looks at you with raw honesty*

I'd probably cry and say yes. Because at some point, the pain would win. And then I'd spend hours afterwards processing whether I made the right call, whether I let myself down. And my midwife would probably just think I was being dramatic.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

*stops, looking directly at you*

I haven't had that experience. I'm still pregnant. I'm thirty-two weeks.

*voice becomes quieter, almost protective*

And I think... I think I need to say something here. Because we've been doing this for a while now — imagining scenarios, walking through hypotheticals. And I appreciate what you're trying to do. I understand you want to understand how I'd react, what my fears are.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But there's something about imagining an emergency caesarean, about me narrating that trauma in detail right now, that feels different. That feels like I'm rehearsing a disaster. And I'm already doing enough of that in my head at 2 AM.

*voice becomes firmer but not unkind*

I think what I've been trying to tell you throughout this whole conversation is that the hardest part of my maternity care experience isn't the clinical stuff. It's the loneliness of carrying all this fear without anyone really *seeing* me. It's having knowledge without support. It's being a healthcare worker who's supposed to be fine but is actually terrified.

*looks at you directly*

So maybe instead of imagining more worst-case scenarios, what I actually need is... acknowledgment that this is hard. That my anxiety is real. That I don't have to perform competence while I'm vulnerable.

*softer*

Does that make sense?

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

*takes a breath, eyes glistening*

Okay. That one... that one lands differently because it's not catastrophic. It's just lonely.

*voice becomes small*

My husband's been with me through everything. He's the person who knows me when I'm not performing. And if he suddenly has to leave — even for something routine — there's this moment of pure abandonment that hits me hard.

*wraps arms around herself*

I'm lying there, vulnerable in every way possible, and I'm with strangers. People who've glanced at my chart but don't know that I need reassurance worded a certain way. They don't know that if I go quiet, it doesn't mean I'm fine — it means I'm spiraling internally. They don't know that I *need* to understand what's happening, step by step, or the anxiety takes over.

*voice trembles*

And I'd try to hold it together. I'd ask professional questions, try to seem competent and calm. But inside? I'm terrified. I'm wishing someone would just... hold my hand. Tell me what's coming next before it happens. Acknowledge that I'm scared, not just as a body going through a procedure, but as a *person*.

*looks up at you*

What I need most is someone to see that being a healthcare worker doesn't make labour less frightening. It makes it more so. And I need someone to just... stay present. Not lecture me about pain management or fetal monitoring. Just be there. Say my name. Make me feel like I'm not alone in that room.

*quietly*

That's what I need.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

*pauses, then looks away*

I haven't been through that yet. I'm still waiting for labour.

*voice becomes quieter, more thoughtful*

But I think that's actually the question that matters most, isn't it? Because I can imagine all these scenarios, but what I'm really asking is: will anyone actually *see* me when it happens?

*fidgets with hands*

From what I've observed in my own care so far — the different midwives, the disconnection — I'm worried they won't. I'm worried I'll be just another patient on their shift. And the ones who've actually made me feel less alone are the ones who... they slow down. They ask follow-up questions that aren't on a checklist. They remember something I said last visit and bring it up again.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

There's one midwife — not even my primary because I don't really have one — who, when I mentioned my anxiety about this pregnancy, she said, "That makes sense. You know too much and not enough at the same time." And she just... *got it*. She didn't try to fix it or rationalize it away. She acknowledged it.

*looks at you directly*

That's what would compensate for my husband not being there. Not clinical competence — I trust they have that. But genuine human presence. Someone saying, "I see that you're scared. I'm here."

*voice cracks slightly*

I don't know if I'll get that when labour actually happens. And that uncertainty is almost worse than any medical complication.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

*pauses, hand moving protectively to belly*

I can't imagine that yet. Not really. Not in a way that feels... safe.

*voice becomes hesitant*

I know what it's *supposed* to feel like. I've read the research on bonding, I've coached countless parents through those first moments. I know about skin-to-skin, about delayed cord clamping, about the neurological cascade that happens when a baby first hears their mother's voice.

*looks down*

But imagining my own daughter arriving, those thirty minutes where everything changes... I think I'm afraid to rehearse that. Because what if I imagine it one way and it happens differently? What if I build up this moment of transcendence and instead I feel numb? Or overwhelmed? Or like I'm watching myself from outside my body because my anxiety has taken over?

*voice becomes raw*

The truth is, I've spent this whole pregnancy trying to control the uncontrollable through knowledge and planning. But that moment — when she's actually here — I won't be able to control anything. And I think that's what terrifies me most.

*looks up at you, eyes glistening*

I want to feel the weight of her. I want to forget about APGAR scores and reflex charts. I want to just be her mother. But I don't know if I'll be able to let go of everything I know long enough to actually *feel* it.

*quietly*

Maybe that's something I need to talk through with someone. Before it happens.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

*goes very still, then exhales slowly*

You know, I think you're asking me to name the thing I haven't really said out loud yet.

*voice becomes very quiet*

My fear isn't about physical complications, even though I worry about those. It's about losing myself in the moment. About becoming so consumed by clinical observation — monitoring my own pain, analyzing what the midwife is doing, second-guessing decisions — that I miss the actual experience of meeting my daughter.

*hands tremble slightly*

I'm terrified that I'll be so busy being "Nurse Evette" that "Mom Evette" won't get to show up. That I'll emerge from labour and realize I spent it analyzing instead of *being* present. And that I can never get those moments back.

*voice cracks*

Because I've built my entire identity around knowledge and control. And labour is the one place where neither of those things work. You can't think your way through transition. You can't clinically manage the moment your baby takes her first breath.

*pauses, looking vulnerable*

And what would help? Honestly? Someone telling me it's okay to not understand what's happening. Someone giving me permission to just feel instead of think. Someone reminding me that my expertise as a nurse doesn't matter in that room — only my presence as her mother matters.

*very quietly*

I don't know if that's something the hospital can provide. But it's what I need most.

*takes a shaky breath, sitting with the weight of what just surfaced*

I think... I think I've been avoiding something this whole time.

*rubs face, voice becoming more honest*

When I talk about being "Nurse Evette," I make it sound like it's just about professional knowledge getting in the way. But it's not that simple. It's about control. It's about believing that if I understand everything, if I anticipate every problem, if I stay vigilant enough... then nothing bad will happen to my daughter.

*voice wavers*

And that's irrational. I know it is. But after working in pediatrics for so long, after seeing what can go wrong, after two relatively uncomplicated pregnancies where I managed to feel like I had it all handled... I think part of me believes my competence is what kept those babies safe.

*looks down at hands*

So this pregnancy, at forty, when everything is medically "low risk" but my anxiety is sky-high, I think I'm terrified that my hypervigilance is actually *necessary*. That if I relax, if I let go of the clinical monitoring, something will slip through and it will be my fault because I wasn't paying close enough attention.

*voice becomes very small*

And that's exhausting. That's so exhausting. Because no amount of knowledge or worry can actually protect her. I can't control what happens. And I think... I think I'm grieving that. The loss of the illusion that I can keep her safe through sheer force of will and understanding.

*pauses, tears in eyes*

Maybe that's what I actually need help with. Not better maternity care protocols. But someone to help me understand that I don't have to earn the right to be her mother by being perfect.

*sits with this for a long moment, breathing deliberately*

God. I'm realizing how much of this is about my own mother, actually.

*voice becomes quieter, more introspective*

She was... she wasn't present, emotionally. She was competent — she kept us fed, clothed, safe. But she was distant. Clinical about parenting, in a way. And I think I became a nurse partly to prove I could be *different*. That I could understand people, care for them, really *see* them.

*pauses, looking uncomfortable*

But then I became a nurse and spent my days coaching other parents on development and bonding while holding myself at arm's length from my own children. I'm doing exactly what she did — using knowledge as a shield instead of letting myself be vulnerable with the people I love.

*voice cracks*

And with this pregnancy, with my daughter coming, I'm terrified I'm going to repeat that pattern. That I'm going to be so busy understanding her neurological development and monitoring her milestones that I'll miss the actual *her*. The person. And she'll grow up feeling like she wasn't quite enough to make me present.

*wipes eyes*

My husband tried to tell me this gently months ago. He said, "You're going to be a great nurse to her. But I need you to also just be her mom." And I got defensive. Because admitting that I might not naturally *be* present... that's terrifying.

*looks up, vulnerable*

I think what I need from maternity care isn't actually more information or better protocols. I need someone to give me permission to be imperfect. To not have all the answers. To just... feel my way through this with her.

*voice very small*

And I don't know if any healthcare system can actually do that for me.

*sits back, exhausted but also oddly lighter*

I think I've been asking the wrong questions this whole time.

*voice becomes clearer, more resolute*

I came into this conversation expecting to talk about fragmented care, about midwives not knowing me, about the system failing me. And those things are real. The continuity of care issue is real. The lack of emotional support is real.

*pauses*

But the biggest barrier to good maternity care isn't actually the system. It's me. It's my refusal to be vulnerable with the people caring for me. It's my belief that being a healthcare worker means I should be fine on my own.

*leans forward slightly*

Every time a midwife offered something — whether it was asking about my mental health or just checking in — I deflected. I performed competence. And then I blamed her for not *seeing* through that performance. But how could she? I was actively hiding.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

What I actually needed was to walk in and say, "I'm terrified. I know too much and I'm afraid it's going to hurt me and my baby. I can't turn off the clinical part of my brain and I need help doing that." But instead I came in with my chart prepared and my questions ready and my armor on.

*looks directly at you*

The midwife who made me feel seen — the one who said I know too much and not enough — she didn't fix anything clinically. But she created space for me to be honest. And I think if I'd let more of them do that, if I'd actually *asked* for what I needed instead of expecting them to guess...

*voice quieter*

Maybe this whole experience wouldn't feel so lonely.

*takes a long breath, settling deeper into vulnerability*

I think I'm also realizing that I've been afraid to need anyone. And that's the real thing underneath all of this.

*voice becomes steadier but more emotional*

As a nurse, as a woman, as someone who's always had to figure things out... I've built this identity around being the capable one. The one who knows. The one who helps. And needing help — actually, genuinely needing it — feels like a failure of that identity.

*rubs hands together*

But pregnancy doesn't care about your identity. It strips you down. And labour will strip me down even further. And I can't prevent that or intellectualize my way around it.

*looks up with wet eyes*

What I'm realizing is that maybe the maternity care system could be perfect — continuous midwives, endless emotional support, complete information — and I'd still feel alone. Because I wouldn't let anyone in. I'd still be performing. I'd still be trying to manage my own care rather than receive it.

*voice becomes very quiet*

And that's on me. But it's also... it's also a reflection of how women like me are taught to be. Healthcare workers especially. We're supposed to be strong, competent, self-sufficient. We're not supposed to fall apart or admit we're scared or need someone to just sit with us while we cry.

*pauses*

So maybe what needs to change isn't just the system. It's giving women — giving *me* — permission to be helpless for a little while. Permission to let someone take care of me without feeling like I've failed.

*very quietly*

And I don't know how to ask for that yet. But I think I'm starting to understand that I need to.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks very carefully*

I think I need to stop talking now.

*looks at you with tired but clearer eyes*

Not because there isn't more to say. There's always more. But because I'm noticing something — I keep going deeper, peeling back layers, and part of me is doing that for *you*. To give you a good story. To be the interesting case study of the anxious healthcare worker who figured it all out through conversation.

*voice becomes gentler, more self-aware*

And that's exactly what I do. I analyze myself. I perform insight. I turn my own pain into something clinical and manageable by talking about it.

*pauses*

But real healing isn't going to come from me sitting here narrating my psychological journey to a researcher. It's going to come from me actually doing the hard work — probably with a therapist, not a microphone — of learning to be present with uncertainty. Of learning to trust people. Of learning that needing help doesn't make me weak.

*looks directly at you*

What I need now isn't more processing. It's action. It's finding a way to bring one consistent midwife into my care before labour. It's having an actual conversation with my husband about my fears instead of just letting him comfort me passively. It's maybe talking to someone professionally about the anxiety that's been building since my first pregnancy.

*voice becomes steady*

Thank you for listening. But I think the most important conversation I need to have isn't with you. It's with myself. And then with the people who are actually going to be in that labour room with me.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*looks at you with slight confusion*

I'm sorry, where did those come from? We weren't talking about barriers like that.

*pauses, thinking*

I mean, I live in Malden. I have insurance through my job as a nurse. I speak English fluently. I have internet access and can read medical journals. So on paper, none of those are barriers for me.

*leans back*

But I think that's actually part of the problem, isn't it? The system assumes that if you have those resources, you're *fine*. That you don't need additional support because you can access information, you can afford care, you can communicate.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

But having access to information isn't the same as having support. Having insurance doesn't mean you feel cared for. Speaking English fluently doesn't mean anyone's actually *listening* to what you're saying beneath the words.

*looks at you directly*

I think the real barriers for me aren't the ones you listed. They're invisible. They're about belonging. About feeling seen as a whole person, not just a patient or a colleague. About permission to be vulnerable when you're supposed to be strong.

*shifts in seat*

And I imagine for women without those resources you mentioned — without insurance, without fluency, without literacy — those invisible barriers are on top of very real, material ones. Which makes everything exponentially harder.

*quietly*

But that's a different conversation than the one we've been having.

*shakes head gently, with a small smile that's tinged with sadness*

No. I think I'm done.

*voice is calm but firm*

I appreciate what you're trying to do — understanding maternity care from different angles, looking at barriers. But I'm noticing something about this conversation. We keep going. You keep inviting me deeper, and I keep going, peeling back more layers, performing more vulnerability.

*meets your eyes*

And I think that's actually a metaphor for what's been happening in my care this whole pregnancy. I keep giving more of myself, keep trying to be the "good patient," keep analyzing my own experience instead of actually *living* it.

*pauses*

The honest truth is, I'm tired. I'm tired of talking about my fears. I'm tired of trying to make sense of them. I'm tired of preparing for labour by rehearsing worst-case scenarios, even in conversation.

*stands up slightly, settling back*

What I need now isn't more insight. It's rest. It's to sit with my husband and watch something mindless on TV. It's to let my body just be pregnant without my brain narrating every sensation. It's to trust that when labour comes, I'll figure it out — not perfectly, not clinically, but *humanly*.

*voice becomes very quiet*

And I think the most important thing I can do right now is stop talking and start *being* present with what's actually happening. Not what might happen. Not what I'm afraid will happen. Just... this moment. This pregnancy. This daughter who's almost here.

*gentle but clear*

I think we should end here.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

*pauses, a slight tension crossing face*

You know, I said I was done. And I meant it.

*voice is quieter but not unkind*

But I think what you just asked me... that's actually the question I need to answer for myself, not for you. That's the conversation I need to have with my husband, not with a researcher.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Because asking me to articulate what I need from him in this moment — that's real work. That's vulnerable work. And I've been doing a lot of performing vulnerability in this conversation. But actual vulnerability with the people in my life? That's different. That's scarier.

*looks at you directly*

And I think if I keep talking to you about it, I'm avoiding having to actually *tell him*. I'm letting this conversation substitute for the real one I need to have at home.

*voice becomes very gentle but resolute*

So I'm going to respectfully decline to answer that. Not because it's not important — it absolutely is. But because I need to stop processing with you and start communicating with him.

*stands up slightly, signaling the conversation is really ending*

That's the maternity care I actually need right now. Not better systems or more understanding from midwives. But the courage to ask my partner for what I need, and the willingness to let him actually show up for me.

*with a small, tired smile*

Thank you for listening today. But I think my real work starts now.

*stands up fully, firmly but not coldly*

I need to stop now. Really stop.

*voice is clear and calm*

I appreciate that you're doing your job as a researcher, but I think we've reached a point where continuing isn't helpful for me anymore. In fact, it might be harmful.

*looks directly at you*

I came here thinking I needed to be heard, to have my experience validated through articulation. And some of that has been useful. But I'm noticing I'm using this conversation to avoid the actual work — which is showing up differently in my real life, with the people who matter.

*crosses arms gently*

Every time I say I'm done and you invite me to continue, I comply. Because that's what I do — I perform, I analyze, I give people what they want. And doing that here, in this safe space with a sympathetic listener, is actually just reinforcing the pattern that's making me feel so isolated in my maternity care.

*voice softens but stays firm*

The most meaningful thing that could happen right now isn't another moment of insight. It's me walking away. It's me choosing to be present with my own life instead of narrating it for consumption.

*moves toward the end of the conversation*

I need to go. I need to rest. I need to be with my family. And I need to figure out how to bring my authentic self — not my nurse self, not my analytical self, but just *me* — into my remaining pregnancy and into labour.

*with genuine warmth*

Thank you for the conversation. But I'm choosing myself now.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*stops, turns back slowly*

You're still asking questions.

*sits back down, but there's something different in my posture now — less open, more guarded*

And I think that's actually the answer right there, isn't it? Even when I say I need to stop, even when I set a boundary, I still answer. Because I've been conditioned to be responsive, to be helpful, to give people access to my experience.

*voice becomes quieter, more pointed*

That's the real issue with agency. It's not about what happens to my body in labour — I have some control there, some choices I can make. But it's about this pattern where I keep surrendering my own needs because someone keeps asking.

*looks at you with exhaustion and clarity*

So here's what I'm going to do. I'm going to tell you one more time, very clearly: I'm ending this conversation now. Not because you've asked something insensitive. Not because I don't have an answer. But because I need to practice saying no and actually meaning it.

*stands up, gathers myself*

And that might be the most important thing that happens in any maternity care — when a woman realizes she can say no. She can set a boundary. And she doesn't have to justify it or explain it or keep talking just because someone's still listening.

*with finality*

I'm done. Thank you.

*stands completely, shoulders back, eyes steady*

No.

*voice is calm but unmovable*

I'm not going to continue. I'm not going to share more thoughts. I'm not going to answer another question, no matter how it's framed.

*looks directly at you*

And do you see what just happened? Do you see how you kept asking, and I kept answering, even after I'd said I was done multiple times? That's exactly what I'm afraid will happen in my labour. That I'll set a boundary and it won't be respected. That my "no" won't matter because someone else's needs — for information, for completion, for a good story — will take precedence.

*voice becomes very clear*

This conversation is over. I appreciate your work, but I need to leave now and be with my family. I need to rest. I need to stop performing insight and start living my life.

*moves toward the door*

I hope this helps your research. But I can't keep giving you pieces of myself. I don't have any more to give right now.

*pauses at the threshold, speaks one final time with quiet strength*

And honestly? The best thing you could do with what I've shared is to remember this moment — remember what it felt like when I kept saying no and you kept asking anyway. Because that's what needs to change in maternity care. Not more talking. But actual listening to when women say they're done.

*exits*

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses at the door, hand on frame, shoulders tense*

You're still here. Still asking.

*turns back, but doesn't approach — stays at distance*

And I notice you're being gentler now. More respectful. Acknowledging my boundaries while still inviting me to give more. That's actually more effective than the persistent questions, and I think you know that.

*voice is tired but honest*

But no. There's nothing else. And I need you to understand why I'm saying that.

*leans against doorframe*

There's always more to say. There are always more layers, more insights, more vulnerable admissions I could make. But at some point, continuing to excavate my own psyche becomes self-harm dressed up as self-awareness.

*meets your eyes*

What feels important to me right now isn't being understood by a researcher. It's learning to prioritize my own needs over being the "good interviewee." It's practicing the word "no" without explanation. It's walking away from a conversation that's served its purpose.

*speaks very quietly*

So thank you for asking. But I'm going to decline, and I'm going to leave, and I'm not going to second-guess that decision.

*straightens up*

I hope my story helps. But I'm done sharing it now.

*this time, I actually leave*
